These are all major trends, and overall, it's a very positive environment. Of course, there are risks: regulatory bills failing, a global recession, or other unforeseen problems. But barring those, I think it will go up significantly by the end of the year, and 2027 will be a pretty good year for crypto. XI. The Clarity Act and its Impact Deribit: How important is the Clarity Act? Matt Hougan: I think this will be a big trigger. The reason is that we are now building a regulatory foundation on sand. The current regulatory environment in the US is very good, but we are only one step away from a strong change of ruling party, which could bring a completely different regulatory environment because it is not a legislative guarantee. It will not go back to the Gensler era, but it may be worse than it is now. This risk is preventing Wall Street from investing more in this space. They are doing a lot now—mergers and acquisitions, building, hiring—but if there were a solid regulatory framework, it would be 2 to 4 times the size it is now. If the bill passes, the rest of the year will be smooth sailing for crypto; if it doesn't, we may retest the lows. Deribit: Circle has doubled in the past month. Is this pricing in a high probability of the Clarity Act passing? Matt Hougan: Yes, Circle has performed exceptionally well. I think it's pricing in the optimism surrounding the Clarity Act, and also in the expectation that it might gain a larger share of the next trillion stablecoins than the existing market. Even at its current price, I think it's still a very interesting stock. Deribit: What are the chances of the Clarity Act passing? Matt Hougan: We paid consultants in Washington, and they told us it's 50/50—I could get that conclusion myself by flipping a coin, I don't know why we paid them. That's just how it is, it's really hard to say. The window is narrowing, and Polymarket's odds are swinging between 65% and 40%. If I had to bet, I'd slightly lean towards it not passing. Washington is very distracted, and it's a complex issue.

    Thirteen,Partisanization of Crypto

    Deribit: Has Bitcoin being seen as a "Trump concept asset" become a negative factor?

    Matt Hougan: You're right, the partisanization of crypto is a risk that could trigger a counter-movement. But I don't think we'll ever go back to the Gensler era—when BlackRock, JPMorgan Chase, Croup, and others were building and investing heavily in this space, that was a huge lobbying force. They won't make stablecoins illegal or completely ban DeFi when Apollo is investing in Morpho tokens. Washington is more realistic than that. But partisanization is indeed a risk, which is why the Clarity Act is so important—it can turn what we called a sandy foundation into concrete, making it difficult to rescind even if politics changes.

data.title: Trump's "last-minute change of heart": Window of easing tensions or an escalation trap?
data.description:
Authors: Gao Shan, Xi Yue, Xinhua News Agency reporters

US President Trump stated on the 23rd that the US and Iran had held "very good and productive" talks over the past two days and announced a postponement of military strikes against Iranian power plants and energy infrastructure. Iran immediately denied any contact with the US.

Analysts believe that Trump's unilateral announcement of "US-Iran contact" and "postponement of action" reflects the multiple pressures facing the US government. Its purpose is, on the one hand, to stabilize oil prices and appease the market, and on the other hand, it may be to buy time to prepare for further escalation of the conflict. The future of the US-Israel-Iran conflict remains highly uncertain.

Have there been talks? US and Iran's statements are vastly different

On the 21st, Trump issued an "ultimatum" to Iran, demanding that Iran open the Strait of Hormuz within 48 hours, or he would destroy "various power plants" in Iran. Iran responded that if the US took action, all energy and oil infrastructure in the Middle East would be considered legitimate targets and irreversibly destroyed.

Before the expiration of the US ultimatum, Trump announced on the 23rd that the US would "delay" the strike on Iranian power plants by five days, and claimed that the US-Iran dialogue was "perfect" and that key points of an agreement had been reached. Multiple US and Israeli media outlets reported that the US was in talks with Iranian Islamic Parliament Speaker Ghalibaf, and several coordinating countries were attempting to arrange a meeting between Ghalibaf, US Presidential Envoy Witkov, Trump's son-in-law Jared Kushner, and US Vice President Vance later this week in Islamabad, Pakistan. However, Iran immediately denied any contact with the US. Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Bagaei said on the 23rd that Iran had not held any negotiations with the US, and that it had received messages from some friendly countries in the past few days regarding the US demand to end the war through negotiations, and had responded appropriately based on its principled position. Ghalibaf himself also denied any negotiations with the US. An article in The New York Times points out that since the outbreak of the US-Israel-Iran war, the conflicting parties have frequently issued contradictory statements to serve their respective agendas. Other US media outlets have stated that due to a lack of clear objectives in the military action against Iran, Trump often makes contradictory statements regarding subsequent actions, sometimes even within a single day. Media and analysts believe that the Trump administration's current softening stance on Iran is mainly due to multiple pressures from both inside and outside the country. First, the ongoing conflict has driven up global oil prices, disrupted financial markets, and exacerbated inflationary pressures in the United States. As of March 23, the average retail price of gasoline in the US was close to $4 per gallon, an increase of more than $1 compared to February 28. The Washington Post reported that the Middle East conflict will cost the US economy, and the Trump administration faces a "vulnerable moment." Some analysts point out that reassuring the market is the core reason for Trump's "change of heart," and this is also a key motivation for Trump's announcement before the New York stock market opened on Monday that the US-Iran contact and strike would be postponed. Relatedly, if the war continues to impact the economy and people's livelihoods, the midterm elections will be affected. On the issue of military action against Iraq, a rift has already appeared between Trump and his voter base, the "Make America Great Again (MAGA)" camp. The Heritage Foundation, a conservative think tank, points out that if the war continues to escalate, the Democratic Party will "take control of Congress" in the midterm elections. Furthermore, America's allies are also "working hard." CNN reported on the 23rd, citing multiple sources, that America's Gulf allies privately warned Trump that the US bombing of Iranian power plants could lead to a "catastrophic escalation," and the Trump administration shifted its stance after weighing the options.

Is there another motive? A delaying tactic or a way to de-escalate?

Various interpretations suggest that the Trump administration's "last-minute change of heart" may have ulterior motives. Some media reports say that Iran believes Trump's remarks are an attempt to "lower energy prices and buy time for his military plans."

Ross Harrison, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, a US think tank, believes that this could be "a way out" to de-escalate or even end the conflict, but "it could also be a trap," namely, as Iran says, Trump buying time to prepare for expanded military action.

Liang Yabin, a professor at the Institute of International Strategic Studies of the Central Party School, analyzed that Trump's move might be a "delaying tactic."

On the one hand, after more than 20 days of airstrikes, the US military may be facing a missile shortage, making it difficult to support large-scale airstrikes and air defense missions. Replenishing and transporting ammunition will take time. On the other hand, the Trump administration may be waiting for reinforcements to be deployed, preparing for potential ground military operations. The Wall Street Journal reported on the 23rd that the US 31st Marine Expeditionary Unit will arrive in the Middle East on the 27th, which happens to be the new deadline set by Trump for Iran to "open" the Strait of Hormuz. Furthermore, Iran believes that the Trump administration's move is intended to create chaos within Iran. Iran's Fars News Agency reported on the 23rd, citing sources, that the US fabricated the false news of negotiations with Ghalibaf to discredit him, incite division within Iran, and stir up public sentiment, while simultaneously creating conditions for his assassination. Ding Long, a professor at the Middle East Institute of Shanghai International Studies University, stated that Iran is currently not at a disadvantage in the conflict, and while its willingness to negotiate is weak, it also faces real difficulties such as escalating arms depletion and economic and livelihood hardship. Conversely, the US stance has softened somewhat, and the situation may see a scenario of fighting while negotiating, or using the conflict to promote dialogue. Ding Long also pointed out that the future of the US-Israel-Iran war remains uncertain, and it cannot be ruled out that the US is feigning détente while actually preparing for war. However, the willingness of countries like Pakistan to mediate is a positive sign, and this conflict can ultimately only be truly ended through diplomatic negotiations.
